topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed stone. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was a notification from the publication, confirming the article I’d poured weeks of my life into—my unique perspective, my meticulously researched arguments—was, in fact, credited to a complete stranger. A stranger who clearly possessed a better pen, or perhaps just a more agreeable public persona. I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting off my suddenly cold eyes. A slow, deliberate movement took over; I picked up my phone, my fingers trembling slightly, and began drafting a reply. Every word I typed was sharp, laced with an acidic edge that I could feel vibrating in my chest. I didn't want an apology; I wanted a public dismantling of this deception. I hit send, the act feeling less like communication and more like an execution.

[story 2]

I found out when I was waiting for the final proof copy, tucked away in a dusty corner of the office. The name on the byline was wrong. It belonged to someone else entirely—a slick, established voice I’d never heard of, someone whose prose felt utterly hollow compared to my own passionate prose. A hot flush crept up my neck, quickly morphing into a deep, simmering resentment. I didn't shout. Instead, I methodically organized my desk, stacking papers with exaggerated precision, ensuring every corner was immaculate. When I finally confronted the editor, my voice was dangerously level, every syllable measured and cold. "This is unacceptable," I stated, my gaze unwavering. "I expect this correction to be made immediately. And I expect a full accounting of how this misattribution occurred." The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken accusations.

[story 3]

The realization hit me during my morning coffee ritual. I had spent hours agonizing over the nuances of my piece, the careful calibration of every sentence. Then, the notification arrived: my work was published, but under another author’s name. A strange, almost physical tightening occurred in my jaw. I took a long, slow sip of the bitter coffee, letting the heat burn a little. My hands, usually steady, clenched around the mug until my knuckles were white. I walked over to the window, looking out at the bustling street, feeling a surge of intense, focused energy. I didn't cry or rage aloud. Instead, I began mentally cataloging every detail of the publication's process, every loophole, every oversight. A quiet resolve settled over me, hardening into something unyielding. I would ensure they understood the cost of such a slight.
